text:
Ben Guo love that you posted this! Storing your "data valuables" is immensely important. 

We should all have our personal little Libraries of Alexandria

Using AI for the job search is an exercise in using it correctly -- and sparingly.

Ideally, it's used to help aggregate opportunities (e.g. Warmer Jobs) and improve the underlying ability of a candidate to advocate for themselves, particularly early career folks (aka what we do at Careerspan).

Conversely, stay far away from auto apply tools (which are glorified spam bots that'll have you applying for both a gardening position in the midwest and an investment banking internship in New York City -- and that's if your name doesn't get flagged in the ATS for submitting junk apps). 

Most resume generation tools are garbage as well because they don't make an effort to get to know the individual first, so anything they produce is necessarily generic or fabricated. That's why folks are better off just using ChatGPT because at least it has memory. 

Hope that helps!

Thank you for the correction Jess Weems Thibault, because I want to be clear about this—paying a recruiter, if they're the right recruiter, is frequently the best path forward, particularly for niche or director-and-up roles. We did it for our last "can't fail" hire and it was 100% the right call. 

Case in point: almost none of the first-time founders I speak to want to pay for recruiters, but I have yet to see a repeat founder who didn't say something to the effect of "I'll pay whoever whatever they want for the right person"

That said, plenty of other roles where the on-demand + coaching-focused nature of Careerspan's offering can give candidates an edge they wouldn't get even with a recruiter!

When I was interning at Gainsight, I took on an effort to figure out why tool adoption among sellers was low. Surveyed maybe 30 or so of the ~80 sellers across all major geographies and product lines. 

A couple of observations:
- Generally, people don't want to learn new tools — even if it makes their life better or easier. 
- Lots of companies suffer from "if we buy it, they will come" syndrome; a lot of thought needs to be put into building the desire—in both individuals and the collective—to use the tool. 
- Having "power users" is key — power users find joy in learning about a product and often have a desire to share that with others. No enablement strategy is better than a really hyped employee recording a loom explaining why tool XYZ will change their lives (coming from the boss doesn't count for the same reason why listening to our parents' advice is harder than listening to a friend's)

Honestly, it's at the point where even the founders I'm speaking to aren't even looking at inbound anymore. 

They hate paying recruiters, but they also don't want to be buried in resumes or screening bad fits.

As a society, we only talk about networking in the context of getting something out of people as opposed to just doing it because you enjoy it.

What most people don't realize: recruiters spent 5 seconds on your resume *to decide* whether they're going to spend an additional 30 seconds on your resume.

That's a very narrow window of opportunity to prove you're worth paying attention to

Tl;Dr: Don't give people even more (stupid) reasons to exclude you from their hiring process

We work on our careers at the exact wrong times!

We try to build a network when we should have one warmed up and ready to go.

We try to update our resume after we've already emotionally given up on our current roles. 

Folks forget that their career is like their health -- an ounce of preventive care beats a pound of emergency treatment every time. 

Hell, that's why we're called Careerspan!

Above all, your writing needs to be accessible when folks are spending under 30s scanning through it. 

Specificity is another underappreciated quality of good professional materials.

We tell folks that you're optimizing for two sets of eyes: robot and human.

There are plenty of tactics that can be used to catch the ATS' attention, but you'll ultimately shoot yourself in the foot by keyword stuffing to the point that it turns off a recruiter. 

The clarity and accessibility of your writing are critical in that regard.

To pour salt on the wounds, you're probably doing yourself more harm by spam applying (and paying 30 to 50 bucks a month for the privilege of doing so) than not.

Sadly, by the time folks realize that "number go up" in a job context doesn't mean squat, they've wasted far too much of their time, money, and attainable opportunities to be happy about the situation.

Almost everyone can stand to hear this. 

Job hunting is a skill unto itself. 

Unfortunately, it's a skill that:
a) We only get to practice for a few months every few years
b) We deploy when we're super stressed, which hampers learning 
c) has poor signal-to-noise when it comes to advice because there's so much out there (a lot of it irrelevant or just plain awful)
d) has an oversized impact on career outcomes relative to its relevance to your actual job 
e) ironically, you are worse at if you're really good at your job or really loyal to your employers because you hone it even less frequently

Increasingly a single data point - or even handful but from the same moment in time - is meaningless. Folks are going to have to have a robust, traceable, provenenced work history that employers can reliably reference

That's genuinely fantastic! Starting with the low-lift, high-impact stuff always helps me tackle situations that seemingly demand overwhelming change. 

Even the mere act of collecting and processing feedback from candidates is such a positive step. We need more "pro-candidate" employers!

I couldn't agree more. 

"If you want to build a ship, don't drum up people to collect wood and don't assign them tasks and work, but rather teach them to long for the endless immensity of the sea."

That said, you need the corresponding personal qualities to channel that ambition correctly. 

In some folks, increased ambition could just as easily translate into playing office politics as it could self directed learning imo.

Until we start seeing the person for the whole of what they are - and not just a mashup of hard skill keywords and credentials - the system is going to say the same. 

We need better candidate data to make better hiring decisions imo.

I'd add that existing capabilities (hard skills) are table stakes. 

In the age of AI - if one buys into it fully - we should be hiring for capacity: capacity to learn and capacity to adapt. 

The rockstar hires of tomorrow are going to be people with the right human qualities to maximally leverage AI. 

Not one-dimensional prompt and workflow jockeys, but rather critical thinkers and maximalist learners.

Careerspan turns conversational coaching for candidates into actionable hiring signals for employers. We help employers hone into the values, mindset, and soft skills that resumes can't communicate but underpin 89% of failed hires. 

TL;DR: Hire for demonstrable qualities, not shoe-horned keywords.

I have a system that essentially recreates a mini computer within each project using specialized prompts within text files and a router in the project level instructions. I found that that approach is best with chatGPT or Claude -- gems can't seem to route to the correct function consistently.

And agreed. Access is a massive issue. Not to mention a lot of career coaches out there are middling at best.

For the sake of your audience though, I'll elaborate briefly.

The essence of good career coaching is guided self-reflection. 

Basically someone that asks you really good questions and helps you come up with the content for your professional narrative and then helps you reconstitute that into strong, targeted talking points 

And that's precisely what we do!
Couldn't agree more! I'm a bit biased, but I can't say there's a better way than Careerspan -- besides paying for an actual career coach, which is always tops if you find the right one.
Thank you Kim Wilkes for inspiring this impromptu post! You helped me hit my quota and now my cofounder Logan Currie won't yell at me.

Self-advocacy is hard, in part because it's easy to feel braggy like Kim Wilkes said.

So, don't brag. 

Treat talking about yourself the same way you would discussing your favorite movie or talking about your favorite sports player or any other topic that you know in depth.

Just share the facts of what you did and the insights you picked up along the way in a detailed and interesting manner -- let the other person pick up what you're putting down. 

Like we say at Careerspan: "you are the world authority on yourself. Now act like it!"

This is going to be one of the central questions in future of work discourse in the coming years.

Our fear is that companies overcorrect and minimize entry-level hiring to such a massive extent that over time institutional knowledge is lost when senior members depart and there are major gaps in managerial capability both at that company and in the economy at large because enough entry level, folks weren't given the chance to rise up the ranks. 

Wise companies, however, will continue to hire a number of highly talented early career professionals and engage with them in a way that engenders long-term loyalty.

I used to sit by myself before interviews, quietly stewing in stress—pretty common, I think. 

When I started spending a few minutes talking to my partner beforehand, I noticed how much more relaxed I was. 

It both took my mind off things and got me warmed up.

You shouldn't have told me that. 

Now you're going to have to deliver a "Stan" style mixtape to Rahul Vohra for me.

Do you use Superhuman? If so, generate in ChatGPT as markdown and then tell the Superhuman AI (Cmd+J) to "format according to markdown and don't touch anything else".

I presume if you have Gemini in your docs, you can do the same thing.

Gokul Rajaram - Tl;Dr: Hard skills (which credentials relate to) are table stakes. We actually need signal into the subjective side of candidates.

We want to hire for qualities, but the current paradigm/technology only allows us to scan for keywords and thus over-rely on proxies. A resume can't *really* tell me if someone's hardworking, so I use the fact that they graduated from an Ivy as a proxy and hope I can validate that in the interview.

News flash: they can't. 46% of hires fail by the 18-month mark. 89% of the reason they fail is a values, mindset, and soft-skills mismatch. ONly 11% was because of their hard skills, which is what credentials are relevant to.

What we need is more high-fidelity candidate signal into their behavioral and psychological side (attained in a scalable way) that allows us to filter based on who someone is, not what credentials they carry.

What are your thoughts on video interviews in general? I'm not a huge fan myself. Putting candidates on the backfoot does more harm than good IMO.

Are y'all working on the "screening at scale" or "identity authentication" problems over at Elly?

Don't go melting my cold, dead heart now, Joe!

(seriously, that's so kind of you to say and it means a whole lot!)

It's often said that you can't prove a negative, but I believe in this context, there are ways in which we can!

you say ChatGPT dependence, I say vibe writing. Who is to say who's correct?

We closely studied Handshake and Garrett Lord's early days when developing our GTM at Careerspan. They wrote *the playbook* on selling to universities -- a famously difficult thing to do. Excited to see how they fare going in this new direction.

At Careerspan, we call that the self-averaging effect.

Our product usage showed that many users perceive themselves as average at things where, from the outside looking in, they were clearly performing at an above-average level. 

To your point, Andrew Yeung, just because something comes easily to you doesn't mean it's necessarily an easy thing to do; it may just mean it's easy *for you*.
Giovanna Ventola if that sounds interesting at all, let's talk. Would love to connect regardless as we have quite a few capabilities that your community might be interested in

Vrijen Attawar Tim He - any job that gets submitted to us for distribution to our community or any job submitted by a user to check their match score first gets run through a literal bullshit detector that pays attention to everything from the specificity of language to obvious markers, such as multiple years of experience for entry-level positions and unpaid work for what is clearly a full-time role. 

Jobs for distribution that fail are kicked from the system. Jobs that user submit that fail have a big old sign that says "red flag alert"  that specifies why you should be wary of that job post. The latter has the dual benefit of both educating the candidate and letting them choose whether or not to proceed with a particular opportunity!

Cost to sound like an awesome, ethical employer: $0
Cost to *be* an awesome, ethical employer: $$$

We actually just added a bullshit detector to our JD matching system to filter out jobs that had many of the red flags you just called out!

That's what I suspected as well. 

It's a shame because they don't realize how much they're shooting themselves in the foot. People are intrinsically bad at absorbing and delivering data simultaneously. 

I can't conceive of a way where even an above-average communicator can process and deliver a scripted answer on the fly unless they're a trained stage actor or spending so much time optimizing for that ability that they'd likely be better off just preparing themselves better to come off effectively in the first place.

Michael Hess  - I love that you are evaluating for for qualities rather than keywords. 

I'd love to know how y'all currently do so. Is it primarily through the interviews or do y'all do skills-based assessments or something along those lines? 

Also, if you're willing to share, how reliable do you think your approach?

Re live cheating: Something that I don't think a lot of these tools haven't baked into their concept is just how bad human beings are at simultaneously absorbing and delivering information.

In fairness, it's incredibly hard to look at a script and deliver it convincingly for anyone that is in a train stage actor. 

I'm confident that in time folks will see that this whole cheating app thing was solving a human problem as though it was a technical problem. 

That said, building an entirely deep faked bot that answers convincingly in your stead may yet work, but only because you've entirely removed the human from the loop.

My follow-up tier list:
- Tailored + proactive note (Best, surfaced interesting takeaways or have next steps planned in response. Shows that they critically engaged with what was discussed)
- Tailored but simple note (Better, shows they critically engaged with the conversation)
- Generic note (clearly cursory, minimal value add)
- No note 
- Bad note (wrong name, incorrect details, inappropriate content)

Samuel Reed - unfortunately, the responsibility doesn't fall on anyone because the incentives aren't there, at least not for the powers that be. Everyone agrees that resumes are incomplete data points, but we have an entire hiring paradigm dominated by billion-dollar companies that have centered their tech on it. They're scarcely about to forego all that capital investment for the sake of idealism. At minimum they won't be the first movers on getting rid of it.

For what it's worth though, my company Careerspan is taking up the responsibility to develop something better. We use career coaching to both help candidates. Better understand their best attributes and get better at expressing those qualities to employers. In the process, the data that we collect, which contains really rich insights into their character and mentality, can be turned into hiring signals that are far more reliable, detailed, and grounded than resume bullets. Crucially, this allows us to assess candidate quality semantically rather than by scanning for keywords. 

Maybe it's crazy, but I truly believe this is the most pro-candidate way of doing things! 

If that's at all, interesting to you, I'm happy to share more!

Totally agree that top of the funnel is what needs fixing -- I would build on that by saying that, imo, the reason candidates turn to these tools is that they've lost faith in the evaluation mechanisms themselves. ATS is unwilling/unable to look at the underlying substance of who someone is beyond the skills and experiences they list on their resume (i.e. their soft skills, value, mindset). The status quo is best suited for folks with a lot of technical skills/measurable hard skills and sucks for everyone else. After all, if you know you're not being given a fair shake or seen for all of what you can bring to the table, the reflex would be to spam applications till something connects. 

That's why we're building a human-centric data model that incorporates those facets. Hoping that we can one day supplant the resume as the go-to source for candidate data that's 10x richer than what you could extract from a single sheet of paper.

I think there are times when I annoy my coworkers where they would rather be dealing with an AI version of me!

On a more serious note, I've set up something similar for myself - not for off-loading decision-making but rather as a way to ensure consistency in my operations and access novel capabilities that would be too time intensive or onerous to otherwise exercise myself e.g vetting key decisions to ensure consistency in strategy, leveraging a custom "socratic tutor" that helps me flesh out my thinking, generating first-pass content for dissemination, etc.

That said, I could never hand-off authority to an AI-Vrijen -- for me, the chokepoint is my own ability to comprehensively share all the necessary context for making major decisions not to mention the effort associated with keeping that information up-to-date with day-to-day changes, both of which are already difficult without putting my company's future in the hands of a machine. 

I also think it's a little ethically sketchy -- I'm in this role because there is the expectation that I bring my best capabilities to the table. To the extent that the buck ultimately stops with me, I couldn't justify abdicating responsibility to the AI entirely.

Jess Weems Thibault if you're already using ChatGPT pro, then take the prompts that you're using in conversations (especially if you're having the same type of conversations regularly) and turn them into dedicated projects and set the prompts as project-level instructions. For example, I have a project dedicated to processing meeting transcripts and generating notes in the specific way that I want. I have another project built just for creating social media and personal branding content (with very specific instructions around how I want my voice and the output to be generated). Not sure if you're already doing that, but if you aren't, try it! It was a big unlock for me. Hope that helps!

This jobs market is an arms race.

Candidates who rarely get past the ATS resort to a "numbers game" approach where Easy Apply/auto-apply tools are attractive. Employers are flooded by applicants and turn to more technology to help them.

It remains to be seen who will actually come out on top, but I suspect what we need is a third way that re-aligns the shitty incentives currently at play.

4) Don't just ask for a referral - Not only are people more likely to say no or ignore you, per the point in the article, they will be in a better position to help you if they know more about you. We're also more likely to help when we have some sort of an emotional connection to someone. Asking for a 15-20 min call to ask questions about their employer/department/industry tends to be a good way to get some time on their calendar. Request a referral once you've established a rapport is much stronger

Brian Bies I appreciate your support. For me, the following elements tend to be key:
1) Prioritized search - start with existing connections that you can easily re-initiate contact with, move on to 2nd degree connections for whom you can receive a warm introduction, and  then turn to cold outreach if necessary. Following this pattern tends to be useful even if you ultimate need cold outreach because it gives you a lot of great practice before the real challenge
2) Personalize outreach - this boils down to finding points of commonality with someone. The more recent the better. Use this as a wedge to grab their attention. More importantly, no one likes a generic message that they think you've sent to a dozen people before
3) Reference relevant parts of your background - Briefly mention the best, most relevant parts of your profile in the email. 1-2 lines should be sufficient. We're more likely to help people we're impressed by.
It is both unsurprising that you've been included and wonderful to see you succeed. Congratulations!

I think it's the height of disingenuousness to say she's equating all Indians with parasites. Her point applies to individuals who take advantage of the social safety net in America and then say that we need more of a "by your bootstraps" attitude while discounting survivorship bias. Immigrants of all stripes are guilty of this. They've had hard lives and achieved a great deal, but it leads them to support a brutally hierarchical society simply because they've made it to the top so why can't everyone else. Reality is not that simple.
Thanks for your comment, Chris. I couldn't agree more, and I do think that the big guns have misread the tea leaves on this one.
